At the time, the e question hanging over the company was existential:could Apple's s creative spark survive the death of its founder? One of Mr Cook's lieutenants was SO miffed at the criticisms that he publicly retorted in201:"Can'tinnovate any more, my ass!"

Adecadeorso later,MrCook may be feelingdéjà vu.On allthree counts-antitrust,Asian competition,the existential question of innovation and g growth-there a parallels between then and now. Competition watchdogs in the EU are d demanding compliance from March 7th with rules that for the f first time breach the "walled garden" which keeps users and developers b bound within Apple's playpen. On March 4th they fined the company €1.8bn ($2bn)fora allegedly stifling competition in music streaming.In America athe DOJ may soon launch eee against Apple.In China,Huawei,domestic mastodon,i is seizing market share.Hanging over everything is then nagging concern,amid a levelling off in iPhone sales, that Mr Cook is missing the chance to pull another rabbit out of the hat with generative a artificialintelligence(genAI).

by10%sincer mid-December, and Microsoft, thanks to gen AI,vaulting past it to become the world's most valuable company,sceptics wonder if Apple is now so dominant tit has lost i its mojo.So jaded is the narrative that many pay ylittle heedtothebuzzabouttheVision Pro, Apple's snazzy-though 1lavishly E priced-mixed-reality headset.What hopes they have eee pinned on the company's annual 1developer conferencein June,whenthey want Mr Cook to announce whizzy gen-AI upgrades s proving that Apple can join the chatbot hypefest. That,though.is not how the company does things.Nors should it be.

Yet you do not have to be eatrue believer to see why Apple may be right to take its time.First,there will be more to gen AI than chatbots.They appear revolutionary. But so far they are just a better (and acciden-prone) wayofputing inaquery andgetting an answer.That is not Apple'sforte."They arefeatures,not products,"中as Horace Dediu,an expert on Apple,puts it.Nor does Apple compete with othertech giants,such as Microsoft, Amazona and Alphabet,to run cloud-computing platforms with AImodels uo which customers can build gen-AI apps. Instead of relying on the cloud,it seems to be working on ways to embed genalinits own devices,bolstering its ecosystem.Since 2017it has been using homemade chip technology called neural engines to handle machine-learning and ai functions its gadgets use behind the scenes.

In late February emerged that it was scrapping its ten-year project to build an Apple car and redirecting gthe engineers towards gen AI.No doubt itis s moving upa gear—though not from an idle start. Apple will reveal nothing gabout its intentions.But one of the options it has is hiding in plain sight::the Vision Pro.The most recent gen-AI  launches,such as OpenAI's Sora, which converts text to video,and Groq, whichs speaks at humanlike speed in response to questions,suggests that eventually something other than written words could be ther main gateway to gen AI. The Vision Pro is all about sounds and images.

In the short term none of this will resolve the growth question.In fact,theregulatory onslaughtinthe EUviathe Digital Markets Ac,which will henceforth apply to big-tech h"gatekeepers"including Apple,could dpotentially crimpits biggest growth engine,services.For the first time Apple will be forced to allow third-party app marketplaces and alternative payment systems outside its Ddc Store on devices in Europe.It has made no secret of its disdain for the rules. It callsthem athreat to safety and privacy,and hasintroduced complex new fees for those who dare bypass its protective walls. Some developers have s slammed its compliance measures,but they are l likely to work:inertia means that many will probably stick with the status quo. As for a possible DOJ antitrust case, it wouldbea headache.But its scope is not yet t clear.

China is a bigger problem with no clears solution.Huawei has becomeaformidable competitor,though in the long run it may be constrained by anA America-led ban on sales toit of high-end chips.However big the geopolitical risks,Apple and China are so co-dependent that they may be stuck with one another.

Still,don't tgive dn on Mr Cook yet. Apple e is bound to be working on gen-AI products that op not leave egg on its face—just, as is its way, notin the open.At this stage, the vast sums needed to train AI models favour deep-pocketed incumbents over scrappyu upstarts, which will workto Apple's advantage.You can almosthear Cupertino muttering,"Can'tinnovate anymore, my ass!"

# Schumpeter | Something's Cooking Apple isrightnottorushheadlongintothegenerative-hefst
1FYOU THINKTimCookhasalwaysledacharmed lifeatthe helm ofAenker Jobs in 2011 wereat trial by fire.First there was antitrust: America's Department of Justice (Doj) sued Apple for conspiring to fix e-book prices.-Then there was competition: Samsung, a South Korean rival,wentto war with the iPhone with bigger,sleeker models.Then camebroader concerns.Apple's new voice assistant,Siri, made rookie errors.Ditto Apple Maps, which went as far as relocating the Washington Monument to the Potomac river. At the time, the e question hanging over the company was existential:could Apple's s creative spark survive the death of its founder? One of Mr Cook's lieutenants was SO miffed at the criticisms that he publicly retorted in201:"Can'tinnovate any more, my ass!" Adecadeorso later,MrCook may be feelingdéjà vu.On allthree counts-antitrust,Asian competition,the existential question of innovation and g growth-there a parallels between then and now. Competition watchdogs in the EU are d demanding compliance from March 7th with rules that for the f first time breach the "walled garden" which keeps users and developers b bound within Apple's playpen. On March 4th they fined the company €1.8bn ($2bn)fora allegedly stifling competition in music streaming.In America athe DOJ may soon launch eee against Apple.In China,Huawei,domestic mastodon,i is seizing market share.Hanging over everything is then nagging concern,amid a levelling off in iPhone sales, that Mr Cook is missing the chance to pull another rabbit out of the hat with generative a artificialintelligence(genAI). by10%sincer mid-December, and Microsoft, thanks to gen AI,vaulting past it to become the world's most valuable company,sceptics wonder if Apple is now so dominant tit has lost i its mojo.So jaded is the narrative that many pay ylittle heedtothebuzzabouttheVision Pro, Apple's snazzy-though 1lavishly E priced-mixed-reality headset.What hopes they have eee pinned on the company's annual 1developer conferencein June,whenthey want Mr Cook to announce whizzy gen-AI upgrades s proving that Apple can join the chatbot hypefest. That,though.is not how the company does things.Nors should it be. Go back to the threat from Samsung in Mr Cook's early days.Back then investors pestered Apple to come dn with a bigger thegen-AIera already has Appleinits sights. Yet you do not have to be eatrue believer to see why Apple may be right to take its time.First,there will be more to gen AI than chatbots.They appear revolutionary. But so far they are just a better (and acciden-prone) wayofputing inaquery andgetting an answer.That is not Apple'sforte."They arefeatures,not products,"中as Horace Dediu,an expert on Apple,puts it.Nor does Apple compete with othertech giants,such as Microsoft, Amazona and Alphabet,to run cloud-computing platforms with AImodels uo which customers can build gen-AI apps. Instead of relying on the cloud,it seems to be working on ways to embed genalinits own devices,bolstering its ecosystem.Since 2017it has been using homemade chip technology called neural engines to handle machine-learning and ai functions its gadgets use behind the scenes. In late February emerged that it was scrapping its ten-year project to build an Apple car and redirecting gthe engineers towards gen AI.No doubt itis s moving upa gear—though not from an idle start. Apple will reveal nothing gabout its intentions.But one of the options it has is hiding in plain sight::the Vision Pro.The most recent gen-AI launches,such as OpenAI's Sora, which converts text to video,and Groq, whichs speaks at humanlike speed in response to questions,suggests that eventually something other than written words could be ther main gateway to gen AI. The Vision Pro is all about sounds and images. ## Known unknowns In the short term none of this will resolve the growth question.In fact,theregulatory onslaughtinthe EUviathe Digital Markets Ac,which will henceforth apply to big-tech h"gatekeepers"including Apple,could dpotentially crimpits biggest growth engine,services.For the first time Apple will be forced to allow third-party app marketplaces and alternative payment systems outside its Ddc Store on devices in Europe.It has made no secret of its disdain for the rules. It callsthem athreat to safety and privacy,and hasintroduced complex new fees for those who dare bypass its protective walls. Some developers have s slammed its compliance measures,but they are l likely to work:inertia means that many will probably stick with the status quo. As for a possible DOJ antitrust case, it wouldbea headache.But its scope is not yet t clear. China is a bigger problem with no clears solution.Huawei has becomeaformidable competitor,though in the long run it may be constrained by anA America-led ban on sales toit of high-end chips.However big the geopolitical risks,Apple and China are so co-dependent that they may be stuck with one another. Still,don't tgive dn on Mr Cook yet. Apple e is bound to be working on gen-AI products that op not leave egg on its face—just, as is its way, notin the open.At this stage, the vast sums needed to train AI models favour deep-pocketed incumbents over scrappyu upstarts, which will workto Apple's advantage.You can almosthear Cupertino muttering,"Can'tinnovate anymore, my ass!"
